mnemonics.title: Try-Catch-Flow
mnemonics.content: Remember: TRY risky code, THROW exception, CATCH and handle. Flow: try→throw→catch. Think 'TTC - Try To Catch'.

mnemonics.title: Exception Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: std::exception → logic_error → invalid_argument, length_error | runtime_error → overflow_error, out_of_range. Remember: LIR (Logic Invalid Runtime) - L for logic, I for invalid, R for runtime.

mnemonics.title: Exception Safety Levels
mnemonics.content: No Throw → Strong → Basic → None (from strongest to weakest). Remember: 'No Sights Beyond The Basic None' or N-S-B-N acronym.

mnemonics.title: Catch Block Order
mnemonics.content: Catch specific exceptions before general ones. Remember: 'Specific Before General' - SBG or 'Handle the Specific before General'.

acronyms.expansion: Run-Time Type Identification - used with catch(Type& obj) for type identification
acronyms.usage: Used by exception handling to determine exception types at runtime

acronyms.expansion: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization - objects acquire resources in constructor
acronyms.usage: Works with exception handling; destructors ensure cleanup during stack unwinding

acronyms.expansion: no exception - specifies function won't throw
acronyms.usage: Used to mark functions that cannot throw, enabling compiler optimizations

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a checkpoint system: try is the checkpoint zone where you monitor, throw is like triggering an alarm, catch is the emergency response team that handles the alarm. Visualize arrows showing flow from throw to matching catch.
Picture unwinding a coiled rope - as exception travels up, each loop (function call) is unwrapped, calling destructors (cleanup) for objects in each loop before reaching the handler.
Draw an inverted tree: std::exception at top, two main branches: logic_error (left) and runtime_error (right), with their leaves being specific exceptions.
Think of exception as a ball thrown up a ladder - it passes through each rung (function) until caught by someone (catch block). Each rung's local objects are destroyed as ball passes.

analogies.analogy: Like a fire alarm system: detectors (try block) sense smoke, alarm triggers (throw), fire department responds (catch). Without handlers, building burns (terminate).
Like leaving a building during fire: you check each room (function call), turn off lights (call destructors), and exit (propagate exception) until you reach safety (catch block).
Like receiving a complaint at one department and forwarding it to the appropriate department - you handle what you can and pass the rest up.
Like a 'No Entry' sign for exceptions - tells the system this function won't cause any exceptions to escape, allowing safer optimizations.
